news_summary: Today's news highlights the vital impact of behind-the-scenes efforts in advancing research and the exciting cultural blend featured in the 2026 Proms with James Bond and prog rock themes.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité d'aujourd'hui met en lumière l'impact crucial des efforts en coulisses pour faire avancer la recherche, ainsi que le mélange culturel passionnant proposé par les Proms 2026, avec leurs thèmes inspirés de James Bond et du rock progressif.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy ponen de relieve el impacto fundamental que tienen los esfuerzos entre bastidores para impulsar la investigación, así como la apasionante mezcla cultural que caracterizará a los Proms de 2026, con temas inspirados en James Bond y el rock progresivo.

articles.title: How hidden contributions power modern research
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 20 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01260-xThe people who work behind the scenes to keep research moving say that there should be more recognition for their roles.
articles.content:
Frank Hemmings collects, catalogues and preserves plant specimens for research.Credit: David Eldridge

In the scorching heat and across the rugged landscapes of southeastern Australia, Frank Hemmings has conducted vegetation surveys for more than 27 years. He has collected and documented thousands of plants from grasslands, forests and some of the nation’s driest regions. As a curator at the John T. Waterhouse Herbarium at the University of New South Wales in Sydney, he carefully dries, identifies and catalogues every specimen, adding each one to a vast archive of plants lining the shelves. His work, which began with roadside field trips to collect weeds, eventually produced specimens that would become indispensable to researchers globally, across a wide range of scientific fields. “I didn’t realize early on how managing a collection of biological specimens means that the samples I collect or identify could lead to work for someone else years down the track,” Hemmings says.

As a scientist in a research-support role, he doesn’t independently write academic papers or conduct experiments. Instead, Hemmings meticulously gathers and preserves the samples that other scientists use to collect data and make discoveries. “I’m not obligated to produce research from these data,” he explains. “My responsibility is to make them available so others can use them in their research.”

Research is powered by technical talent — and recognition is finally on the rise

Hemmings has collected 3,782 specimens and helped to identify more than 10,500. The leaves that he has pressed have contributed to studies in fields such as pharmaceutical chemistry, phylogenomics, ecology and climate change. “Even though I’m listed as a co-author on 27 papers,” he says, “it blew me away to learn that at least 93 other studies were built on the thousands of specimens I collected and identified.” He says that sharing specimen data and metadata is the core of his mission. He vividly recalls a tense discussion with a representative of his university’s legal department, who wanted to impose strict restrictions on distributing collected specimen data. He responded with resolve: “You have to understand — this goes against the very essence of science.”

Hemmings is part of a vast number of research-support specialists working at scientific institutions around the world, often in the shadows. As the ‘stagehands’ of science, they are mostly invisible to the audience but essential to the show. Even though their fingerprints are all over many data sets, they’re rarely recognized as full contributors on projects, as co-authors and in other ways that are formally rewarded by the scientific establishment. In publications, they often appear only in a short phrase in the acknowledgements section. Their hidden labour and expertise can be difficult to measure.

Simon Hettrick, the chair of the Hidden REF initiative, a campaign launched at the University of Southampton, UK, in 2020 to highlight and celebrate these crucial roles, says that “this lack of recognition translates into significant difficulties for people in these roles: in getting support, finding positions and progressing their careers”.

Nature’s careers team interviewed research-support professionals to hear how their work helps to shape the course of modern science, and how recognition — or the lack of it — has influenced their careers.

Hidden hands

“I knew from the outset that I wasn’t born to be that kind of scientist who lives between papers and peer reviews,” says Marten Schöle, a fossil preparator at the Berlin Natural History Museum. As a teenager, he was drawn to the craft of fossil preparation. Schöle made an early, deliberate choice to step away from academic research, which he found overly narrow because scientists often spend years focused on a single question or fossil. At the Walter-Gropius-Berufskolleg, a vocational college in Bochum, Germany, he trained for three years to become a fossil technician. “What keeps me going in this career is the freedom to explore an extraordinary range of fossils and time periods — from a tiny Cambrian worm that lived half a billion years ago to hominin remains a few thousand years old.” That diversity, Schöle says, brings constant excitement and a renewed sense of discovery.

Schöle’s work extends beyond the museum walls. Travelling with field teams, he uncovers and preserves fossils so that they can be transported to Berlin: crocodile-like remains unearthed in Sudan’s deserts, ancient whales and manatees in Iran and amphibians from the Permian period in Russia. Back in the laboratory, he reconstructs and restores the fossils and creates precise 3D models for research. His work demands absolute focus — a single blunder can destroy fossils that nature has preserved for thousands of millennia. He recalls when the skull of an extinct cave bear (Ursus spelaeus) once slipped from his desk and shattered on the floor. “I felt my heart stop,” he says. For four months, he painstakingly glued every fragment back together, often late into the night.

Fossil preparator Marten Schöle (left) contributes crucial support to palaeontology research.Credit: Faysal Bibi

Tech support

Philippa Broadbent also embraces working in a supporting role, but she is a member of an emerging generation of research-support specialists who are fluent in coding, software and data. “Research software engineering is still taking shape,” says Broadbent, a software engineer at the University of Southampton. She describes herself and others in similar roles as the people who improve the accuracy, reproducibility and scalability of research by building sustainable, well-engineered software and teaching researchers how to use and develop essential programs. “The term only appeared about 14 years ago, and it’s up to us to define the future of this field.”

While doing a PhD in psychology, Broadbent joined a journal club analysing reproducibility by looking at scientific literature. “That’s when I realized how central software is to making research transparent and reproducible,” she recalls. That insight ultimately led to her current career. She works on research projects across diverse, often unrelated scientific fields, which demands the continuous adaptation of her tools to meet each researcher’s needs. “The most daunting part,” she explains, “is diving into a completely new area and learning fast enough to support the research effectively”.

Time to celebrate science’s ‘hidden’ contributors

Harry Biddle provides support for research at the Stockholm Environment Institute (SEI) as a data engineer. “I’ve always loved being around researchers in that environment of discovery, but I always hesitated to become a researcher myself,” he says. After earning a master’s degree in mathematical modelling and scientific computing at the University of Oxford, UK, he considered doing a PhD but was drawn more to software programming and design than to academia. Between 2011 and 2015, Biddle worked on software tools for fluid and ocean simulations that were used in famous films, including Interstellar, Les Misérables and films in the Batman and Marvel franchises.

Now, he makes the tools behind the SEI’s Trase project, which tracks the international trade in commodities, such as palm oil, that contribute to tropical deforestation. “I build all the infrastructure that researchers rely on for their research,” including platforms to store and transform data and software tools to build supply-chain models, he says.

Assisting the animal world

Research support can even be life-saving. “Many researchers don’t realize what goes on behind the scenes for us to deliver the results we do,” says Sharon Price, a baboon colony manager at the Southwest National Primate Research Center in San Antonio, Texas. She continuously monitors the colony and aligns its care with ongoing research needs. The animals that she looks after have an important role. “They are our first line of defence in understanding and managing human diseases,” because scientists can observe how pathogens behave in primates.

Sharon Price cares for a baboon colony.Credit: Texas Biomedical Research Institute

Not all support specialists come from an academic background. Fiona Lynch worked in underwater construction for nearly two decades before she became a remotely operated vehicle (ROV) specialist at the Ocean Tracking Network at Dalhousie University in Halifax, Canada, in 2022. “I always wanted to contribute to marine science,” she says. “I realized I could combine my technical skills with something meaningful.” The hands-on skills she developed from surveying pipelines and working with underwater tools and equipment helped her to pilot and maintain a fleet of ROVs used to study deep-ocean ecosystems. Lynch has operated ROVs capable of descending to 1,000 metres, but piloting them was never simple. Shifting weather, strong currents and the occasional equipment failure tested both her skill and her nerve. “In such circumstances, getting the ROV back is a real tug of war,” she admits. “If these vehicles don’t return, the scientists can’t access their data.”

Unseen and uncredited

Because they work behind the scenes, and their efforts are rarely visible beyond their immediate teams, research-support personnel can seem detached or isolated from the broader research community. Everyone on a film set is mentioned in the credits, but the same cannot be said for people such as Schöle. He often hears the stereotype that fossil preparators are grumpy or withdrawn. But he explains that long hours bent over a microscope, using a fine needle on fragile specimens, demand intense concentration and mental endurance. “It’s simply mental exhaustion,” he says. “We just need some solitude to rest and recharge.”

Broadbent notes that there is a similar misconception in her field: “People assume research software engineers work alone, isolated from everyone,” she says. “In reality, it’s a highly collaborative job — we constantly interact with researchers and others across the research community.”

Despite close collaboration with academics, many of these specialists struggle with their professional identity. Even those who have completed postgraduate degrees often question how they fit into the conventional hierarchy of scientific positions. Many feel that if they are contributing to research, then their names should appear on the papers that use their work. If their accomplishments carry the pulse of discovery, then they shouldn’t be treated as merely equipment operators, support specialists say. This lack of acknowledgment has consequences; it can foster resentment between researchers and support personnel, lower morale and stifle collaboration. And of course, it could make career advancement for people in support roles more difficult.

Why science needs more research software engineers

Research-support professionals say that it’s time to start bridging the divide. “There may be academics who view research software engineers as less important, or see their work as a lesser career compared with the traditional academic path,” Broadbent says. Changing that perception, she adds, requires advocacy. “We still have to convince decision makers at the top of scientific institutions that our work is critical.” Schöle knows the feeling well: “You’re often seen as low in the pecking order and not always allowed to speak up when something isn’t right,” he says. Although his relationships with researchers are mostly positive, many younger scientists, he notes, don’t fully understand or value the preparator’s contribution.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the vital yet often unrecognized contributions of research-support professionals who enable scientific discoveries across many fields. It emphasizes the broad positive impact of their work on research progress and calls for greater recognition and career support, which can improve morale, collaboration, and scientific advancement on a large scale.
articles.personality_title: Unsung heroes behind science: the vital work of research-support specialists
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many scientific discoveries depend on people who work behind the scenes, supporting research by collecting samples, preparing fossils, writing software, or caring for research animals. These specialists often do not get the recognition or credit they deserve.

**What happened** – The article shares stories from research-support professionals worldwide, such as Frank Hemmings in Australia who collects plant specimens, Marten Schöle in Germany who prepares fossils, and Philippa Broadbent in the UK who develops software for research. They explain how their careful work helps scientists make new discoveries but often goes unnoticed in formal acknowledgments or author lists.

**Impact** – These specialists provide essential skills that allow research to happen across many fields, from climate studies to medicine. Their work supports studies that can take years to develop, and their efforts are often the foundation for important scientific papers. However, because they are not always listed as authors or fully recognized, they face challenges in career growth and feel undervalued.

**What's next step** – Campaigns like the Hidden REF initiative are raising awareness about the importance of research-support roles. Specialists and supporters are calling for better recognition, clearer career paths, and more respect from scientific institutions. This could lead to more collaboration, higher morale, and new policies that include these professionals as key contributors.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Research-support specialists play a crucial but often invisible role in science, and recognizing their contributions can strengthen research and careers.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les héros méconnus de la science : le rôle essentiel des spécialistes du soutien à la recherche
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses découvertes scientifiques dépendent de personnes qui travaillent en coulisses, en soutenant la recherche par la collecte d’échantillons, la préparation de fossiles, la création de logiciels ou les soins aux animaux de recherche. Ces spécialistes ne reçoivent souvent pas la reconnaissance ou les crédits qu’ils méritent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’article raconte les histoires de professionnels du soutien à la recherche dans le monde entier, comme Frank Hemmings en Australie qui collecte des spécimens de plantes, Marten Schöle en Allemagne qui prépare des fossiles, et Philippa Broadbent au Royaume-Uni qui développe des logiciels pour la recherche. Ils expliquent comment leur travail minutieux aide les scientifiques à faire de nouvelles découvertes, mais reste souvent invisible dans les remerciements ou les listes d’auteurs.

**Impact** – Ces spécialistes apportent des compétences essentielles qui permettent la recherche dans de nombreux domaines, des études climatiques à la médecine. Leur travail soutient des études qui peuvent durer des années, et leurs efforts sont souvent la base d’articles scientifiques importants. Cependant, leur absence fréquente dans les auteurs entraîne des difficultés pour leur carrière et un sentiment de sous-estimation.

**Prochaine étape** – Des campagnes comme Hidden REF sensibilisent à l’importance des rôles de soutien à la recherche. Les spécialistes et leurs soutiens demandent une meilleure reconnaissance, des parcours professionnels clairs et plus de respect de la part des institutions scientifiques. Cela pourrait favoriser plus de collaboration, un meilleur moral et de nouvelles politiques qui reconnaissent ces professionnels comme contributeurs clés.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les spécialistes du soutien à la recherche jouent un rôle crucial mais souvent invisible en science, et reconnaître leurs contributions peut renforcer la recherche et les carrières.
articles.personality_title_es: Los héroes invisibles de la ciencia: el papel vital de los especialistas en apoyo a la investigación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas investigaciones científicas dependen de personas que trabajan detrás de escena, apoyando la investigación mediante la recolección de muestras, la preparación de fósiles, el desarrollo de software o el cuidado de animales de estudio. Estos especialistas a menudo no reciben el reconocimiento ni el crédito que merecen.

**Qué pasó** – El artículo comparte historias de profesionales de apoyo a la investigación en todo el mundo, como Frank Hemmings en Australia que recolecta plantas, Marten Schöle en Alemania que prepara fósiles, y Philippa Broadbent en el Reino Unido que crea software para la investigación. Explican cómo su trabajo cuidadoso ayuda a los científicos a hacer nuevos descubrimientos, pero a menudo queda fuera de las listas de autores o agradecimientos.

**Impacto** – Estos especialistas aportan habilidades esenciales que permiten la investigación en muchos campos, desde el clima hasta la medicina. Su trabajo sostiene estudios que pueden durar años y es la base de importantes artículos científicos. Sin embargo, al no ser siempre reconocidos como autores, enfrentan dificultades para avanzar en sus carreras y se sienten poco valorados.

**Próximo paso** – Iniciativas como Hidden REF están aumentando la conciencia sobre la importancia de estos roles. Los especialistas y sus defensores piden mejor reconocimiento, caminos profesionales claros y más respeto de las instituciones científicas. Esto podría fomentar mayor colaboración, mejorar el ánimo y crear políticas que los incluyan como contribuyentes clave.

**Mensaje en una frase** – Los especialistas en apoyo a la investigación desempeñan un papel crucial pero invisible en la ciencia, y reconocer su trabajo puede fortalecer la investigación y sus carreras.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sunlit, rugged Australian landscape with a large, open wooden cabinet filled with meticulously arranged pressed plant specimens on shelves, alongside a delicate fossil embedded in stone resting on a workbench, and a gentle mechanical arm symbolizing a remotely operated vehicle tenderly holding a small oceanic creature model—all rendered in natural, soft earth tones and subtle blues, with soft glowing light emphasizing the care and hidden dedication behind scientific discovery.

articles.title: James Bond and prog rock to feature in 2026 Proms
articles.summary: Concerts celebrating Benjamin Britten, Steve Reich and Miles Davis also appear in the programme.
articles.content: Eight years after the BSO Resound became the first disabled-led ensemble to perform at the Proms, the Paraorchestra will present an "immersive take" on Steve Reich's Music for 18 Musicians at Bristol's Beacon Hall. It will mark 50 years since the piece premiered.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the Paraorchestra, a disabled-led ensemble, performing at a major cultural event, promoting inclusivity and representation in the arts, which has broad societal significance and positive impact.
articles.personality_title: Paraorchestra to perform Steve Reich’s classic at 2026 Proms
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Proms is a famous annual music festival in the UK that features a wide range of concerts, from classical to modern music. It is known for celebrating great composers and new talent.

**What happened** – In 2026, the Paraorchestra, a music group led by disabled musicians, will perform Steve Reich’s piece called Music for 18 Musicians at the Proms. This performance will take place at Bristol’s Beacon Hall and marks 50 years since the piece was first played.

**Impact** – The Paraorchestra’s role at the Proms shows how important it is to include musicians with disabilities in big cultural events. It helps more people see that everyone can contribute to the arts, no matter their abilities. This kind of representation encourages fairness and inspires others.

**What’s next step** – The Paraorchestra’s performance could open doors for more disabled-led groups to take part in major events. It may also encourage music festivals to include a wider variety of artists and styles in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Paraorchestra’s 2026 Proms performance highlights progress in making music events more inclusive and celebrates a classic piece’s 50th anniversary.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Paraorchestra jouera le classique de Steve Reich aux Proms 2026
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les Proms sont un célèbre festival de musique annuel au Royaume-Uni, qui présente un large éventail de concerts, de la musique classique à la musique moderne. Il célèbre de grands compositeurs et de nouveaux talents.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2026, la Paraorchestra, un groupe de musiciens handicapés, interprétera Music for 18 Musicians de Steve Reich aux Proms. Cette représentation aura lieu au Beacon Hall de Bristol et marquera les 50 ans de la première exécution de cette œuvre.

**Impact** – La présence de la Paraorchestra aux Proms montre l’importance d’inclure les musiciens handicapés dans les grands événements culturels. Cela aide à montrer que tout le monde peut contribuer aux arts, quelles que soient ses capacités. Cette représentation encourage l’équité et inspire les autres.

**Prochaine étape** – La performance de la Paraorchestra pourrait ouvrir la voie à plus de groupes dirigés par des personnes handicapées dans les grands événements. Cela pourrait aussi encourager les festivals de musique à inclure une plus grande variété d’artistes et de styles à l’avenir.

**Une phrase clé** – La performance de la Paraorchestra aux Proms 2026 met en lumière les progrès vers plus d’inclusion dans les événements musicaux et célèbre le 50e anniversaire d’une œuvre classique.
articles.personality_title_es: La Paraorchestra interpretará el clásico de Steve Reich en los Proms 2026
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los Proms son un famoso festival anual de música en el Reino Unido que presenta una gran variedad de conciertos, desde música clásica hasta moderna. Es conocido por celebrar grandes compositores y nuevos talentos.

**Qué pasó** – En 2026, la Paraorchestra, un grupo musical dirigido por músicos con discapacidad, interpretará la pieza Music for 18 Musicians de Steve Reich en los Proms. Esta actuación será en el Beacon Hall de Bristol y marcará los 50 años desde que se estrenó la obra.

**Impacto** – La participación de la Paraorchestra en los Proms muestra la importancia de incluir a músicos con discapacidad en grandes eventos culturales. Ayuda a demostrar que todos pueden contribuir a las artes, sin importar sus habilidades. Esta representación fomenta la igualdad y motiva a otros.

**Próximo paso** – La actuación de la Paraorchestra podría abrir puertas para que más grupos dirigidos por personas con discapacidad participen en eventos importantes. También podría animar a los festivales a incluir una mayor variedad de artistas y estilos en el futuro.

**Una frase clave** – La actuación de la Paraorchestra en los Proms 2026 destaca el avance hacia una mayor inclusión en eventos musicales y celebra el 50º aniversario de una pieza clásica.
articles.image_prompt: An abstract, warm-toned painting of a grand concert hall interior with elegant silhouettes of diverse musical instruments arranged in a circle, featuring subtle outlines of a film reel and a vintage synthesizer intertwined with flowing musical waves, symbolizing the fusion of James Bond themes, prog rock, and Steve Reich’s minimalist composition, all bathed in soft natural hues of amber, ochre, and muted blues.
